content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

message.content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the prompt file to understand the exercise.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### JSON Schema (normative)
237	
238	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240	
241	**Input (stdin):**
242	
243	```json
244	{
245	  "type": "object",
246	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247	  "properties": {
248	    "customer": {
249	      "type": "object",
250	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251	      "properties": {
252	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253	      }
254	    },
255	    "steps": {
256	      "type": "array",
257	      "items": {
258	        "oneOf": [
259	          {
260	            "description": "Quote step",
261	            "required": ["op", "items"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
264	              "items": {
265	                "type": "array",
266	                "items": {
267	                  "type": "object",
268	                  "required": ["type"],
269	                  "properties": {
270	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
272	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274	                  }
275	                }
276	              }
277	            }
278	          },
279	          {
280	            "description": "Claim step",
281	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282	            "properties": {
283	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285	              "incident": {
286	                "type": "object",
287	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288	                "properties": {
289	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
290	                  "damages": {
291	                    "type": "array",
292	                    "items": {
293	                      "type": "object",
294	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295	                      "properties": {
296	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298	                      }
299	                    }
300	                  }
301	                }
302	              }
303	            }
304	          }
305	        ]
306	      }
307	    }
308	  }
309	}
310	```
311	
312	**Output (stdout):**
313	
314	```json
315	{
316	  "type": "object",
317	  "required": ["results"],
318	  "properties": {
319	    "results": {
320	      "type": "array",
321	      "items": {
322	        "oneOf": [
323	          {
324	            "description": "Quote result",
325	            "required": ["premium"],
326	            "properties": {
327	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328	            }
329	          },
330	          {
331	            "description": "Claim result",
332	            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333	            "properties": {
334	              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335	              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336	            }
337	          }
338	        ]
339	      }
340	    }
341	  }
342	}
343	```
344	
345	### Schema example
346	
347	Stdin:
348	
349	```json
350	{
351	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352	  "steps": [
353	    {
354	      "op": "quote",
355	      "items": [
356	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357	      ]
358	    },
359	    {
360	      "op": "claim",
361	      "policy": 0,
362	      "incident": {
363	        "cause": "fire",
364	        "damages": [
365	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366	        ]
367	      }
368	    }
369	  ]
370	}
371	```
372	
373	Stdout shape:
374	
375	```json
376	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377	```
378	
379	## Expected Output Files
380	
381	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382	- Test files
383	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384	
385	## Constraints
386	
387	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
388	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
391

# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### JSON Schema (normative)

The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.

**Input (stdin):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
  "properties": {
    "customer": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
      "properties": {
        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
      }
    },
    "steps": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote step",
            "required": ["op", "items"],
            "properties": {
              "op": { "const": "quote" },
              "items": {
                "type": "array",
                "items": {
                  "type": "object",
                  "required": ["type"],
                  "properties": {
                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim step",
            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
            "properties": {
              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
              "incident": {
                "type": "object",
                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
                "properties": {
                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
                  "damages": {
                    "type": "array",
                    "items": {
                      "type": "object",
                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
                      "properties": {
                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
                      }
                    }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Output (stdout):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["results"],
  "properties": {
    "results": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote result",
            "required": ["premium"],
            "properties": {
              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim result",
            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
            "properties": {
              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

## Test List Created

**Feature**: MHPCO Claim Office (quote + claim operations, CLI)
**Test File**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_08-52-17_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`
**Example Mapping**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_08-52-17_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/example-mapping/claim-office.md`
**Tests**: 40 (all `it.todo()`)

**Test groups (ordered simple → complex)**:

1. quote — base price list and processing fee (5 tests): empty list, sword, amulet, staff, potion
2. quote — component building block (6 tests): 2/3/4/7 runes, mixed types, two-block scenario
3. quote — modifier thresholds (5 tests): loyalty at/below 2y, high-enchant at/below 5, combined cursed+high-enchant
4. quote — modifier scope on multi-item policies (1 test): cursed sword + plain amulet (E7)
5. quote — integration examples (2 tests): newcomer cursed sword (E31, 165 G), long-standing 2nd contract (E32, 160 G)
6. claim — standard reimbursement (2 tests): regular sword damage 500→400, rune damage 200→100
7. claim — enchantment & dragon-material clauses (4 tests): E11, E15, E16, E17
8. claim — deductible per damage event (2 tests): mixed-type dragon attack, two-sword dragon attack
9. claim — insurance sum and cap (4 tests): cap formula, cap unaffected by premium modifiers, block doesn't reduce sum, cap exhaustion across claims
10. rounding (2 tests): premium 197.5→198, payout 350.5→350
11. CLI — JSON in/out (3 tests): basic round-trip, quote+claim sequencing, follow-up tracking
12. CLI — error handling (4 tests): unknown item type, claim references unknown item, negative damage, too-many damages

**Key resolutions captured in example-mapping**:
- "Alike components" → same TYPE (E5, E6)
- Cursed surcharge → per-item, not policy-wide (E7)
- Block requires exactly 3 of same type — does NOT decompose larger groups (7 runes → 7×25=175, confirmed by spec)
- First-insurance surcharge → per item every quote, regardless of customer history (E32)
- All error paths from spec covered with CLI tests (E28, E29, E30, E20)

**Next Step**: Proceed to Red phase to activate the first test.

content.text:
All 5 tests still pass.

**Refactored Code Mass**:
```
function quote(_customer, items): number {
  if (items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;
  return PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN[items[0].type];
}
```

Component Count (function body):
- Constants: 1 (literal 0) = 1
- Bindings: `items` (×2), `PROCESSING_FEE`, `PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN` = 4
- Invocations: `.length`, `items[0]`, `.type`, lookup = 4 → 8
- Conditionals: 1 = 4
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

Total Mass: ~17 (slight increase due to one extra property access, but eliminated a binding and a type cast)

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Changed `Item = Record<string, unknown>` to `interface Item { type: string }`. The type now self-documents that items have a `type` field. This eliminates the `as string` cast — a code smell where TypeScript's type system was being bypassed.
- Removed intermediate `itemType` variable since it's now used only once and the property access is clear without a cast.

**Trade-off accepted**: Mass went up trivially (15 to 17) but clarity improved significantly. The Item interface now expresses what the code expects, and the unsafe cast is gone. Rule 2 trumps APP.

**Anticipation check**: The Item interface only declares `type` — no other fields. This matches exactly what current tests use (`{ type: "sword" }`, etc.). Future fields (enchantment, material, cursed) will be added when their tests demand them. No anticipation.

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- Refactoring: Replaced loose `Record<string, unknown>` with focused `Item` interface; removed unsafe `as string` cast and now-redundant intermediate variable
- Mass change: ~15 to ~17 (clarity prioritized over APP per Rule 2)
- Tests: All 5 passing

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Naming Evaluation**: All names (`quote`, `quoteRunes`, `PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN`, `PROCESSING_FEE`) clearly reveal intent. No renaming needed.

**APP Mass**: Unchanged. The chosen refactor was a comment-only change, which doesn't affect runtime mass.

New comment correctly documents all three explicit cases and the rounding rationale for 7 runes (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).

**Refactorings Considered and Rejected (Anticipation Risk)**:
- Converting `quoteRunes` conditionals to a lookup table (e.g. `{2: 60, 3: 71, 4: 115, 7: 198}`): would generalize for future rune counts no test demands. Rejected.
- Unifying `quoteRunes` and `PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN` into one lookup: obscures that these are two categorically different lookups (count vs type) and adds complexity without test demand. Rejected.
- Extracting a `firstItemType(items)` helper: only one call site exists; extracting now would add an element (Rule 4 violation) without removing duplication.

**Why Minimal Refactoring**: Upcoming tests (mixed-type item lists with "blocks", e.g. "3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks") will force structural restructuring of how `quote` handles items. Refactoring the current rune-specific branches to a more general structure now would either (a) anticipate that future structure or (b) be thrown away. Better to keep the current implementation honestly minimal until those tests force the right abstraction.

**Tests**: All 9 passing.

The current implementation is essentially a series of explicit branches and a small lookup table. Each branch corresponds to a single activated test. Adding generalization, helpers, or a unified pricing engine would anticipate the loyalty/enchantment/curse/cap mechanics still in `it.todo()`. The "no anticipation" rule wins here — code stays small and honest about what it has proven.

content.text:
All 11 tests still pass.

**Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass for `quote`**

```typescript
export function quote(_customer: Customer, items: Item[]): number {
  if (items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;
  if (items.some((item) => item.type === "moonstone")) {
    return quoteWithMoonstone(items.length);
  }
  const itemType = items[0].type;
  if (itemType === "rune") return quoteRunes(items.length);
  return PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN[itemType];
}
```

Component count for `quote`:
- Constants: `0`, `"moonstone"`, `0`, `"rune"` = 4 (down from 7)
- Bindings: `_customer`, `items`, `item`, `itemType` = 4
- Invocations: `.length` (x3), `.some()`, `.type`, `quoteWithMoonstone()`, `[0]`, `.type`, `===` (x2), `quoteRunes()`, `[itemType]` = ~11
- Conditionals: 3 (down from 4)
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

`quote` mass: 4 + 4 + 22 + 12 + 0 + 0 = 42 (slightly different element count, similar total)

New `quoteWithMoonstone` helper adds:
- Constants: `6`, `137`, `88` = 3
- Bindings: `itemType` = 1
- Conditionals: 1 → 4

**Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass for `quote`**

```typescript
export function quote(_customer: Customer, items: Item[]): number {
  if (items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;
  if (items.some((item) => item.type === "moonstone")) {
    return quoteWithMoonstone(items.length);
  }
  const itemType = items[0].type;
  if (itemType === "rune") return quoteRunes(items.length);
  return PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN[itemType];
}
```

- Bindings: `_customer`, `items`, `item`, `itemType` = 4
- Invocations: `.length` (x3), `.some()`, `.type`, `quoteWithMoonstone()`, `[0]`, `.type`, `===` (x2), `quoteRunes()`, `[itemType]` = ~11
- Conditionals: 3 (down from 4)

- Assignments: 0

input.prompt:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 17
Recent changes: Added hardcoded branch for [cursed sword, plain amulet] → 231.

The code has accumulated many hardcoded branches. Consider whether a unified per-item premium computation (base + first-ins per item, curse/high-enchant surcharges, fee) better satisfies Rule 3 (No Duplication) for the 17 currently passing tests. Pricing data per spec: sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40, rune/moonstone 25. Block (exactly 3 of same component type) = 60 base. First-ins = 10% of base (per item, even for follow-up customers). Loyalty (years>=2) = -20% of policy base. High-enchant (level>=5) = +30% of item base. Cursed = +50% of item base.

## Refactor Phase Report

**Naming Evaluation**:
- `quote`: Clear and reveals intent — kept
- `quoteRunes`, `quoteWithMoonstone`: Still describe their purpose, kept as-is (renaming would not improve since they target specific item-type families)
- `PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN`: **Misleading** — the table was used as a generic fall-through, not strictly for newcomers. Eliminated by replacing the lookup with a derived computation `plainSingleItemPremium`.

**Anticipation Check (critical)**:

The user suggested a unified per-item premium engine (`base + first-ins + curse/high-enchant surcharges + loyalty + fee`) covering all 17 active tests. I evaluated this against not-yet-active tests:

- Test todo `"newcomer with a cursed sword (steel, enchant 3), 0 years → 165 G"`: `100 + 50 + 10 + 5 = 165`. A unified engine that applies the curse surcharge per item would compute this immediately. **That is anticipation** — it would make the next Red phase see a passing test and collapse the TDD signal.

Decision: **Rejected the full unification**. Chose a smaller refactoring that improves clarity without changing branch coverage.

**Refactorings Applied**:
1. Extracted `BASE_PRICES` table containing the per-item base price (sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40) — Rule 2 (reveals intent, makes the underlying pricing data explicit).
2. Extracted `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`, `HIGH_ENCHANT_THRESHOLD = 5` as named constants — Rule 2 (replaces magic numbers in conditionals with named thresholds).
3. Replaced the `PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN` lookup table with `plainSingleItemPremium(type)` that derives `base + base*0.1 + fee` from `BASE_PRICES` — Rule 3 (removes duplicated knowledge: previously `BASE_PRICES` values and the `+ 10% + 5` formula were both encoded separately in `PREMIUMS_NEWCOMER_PLAIN`).
4. Replaced `2`, `5` magic thresholds in `if (customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2)` and `if (enchantment >= 5)` with named constants — Rule 2.

**What I deliberately did NOT do** (would anticipate):
- Did NOT unify the hardcoded 231, 95, 145, 195 returns into a per-item summation engine — that would compute the cursed-sword (no enchant, newcomer) case = 165 immediately, anticipating the next Red.
- Did NOT extract a `applyModifiers(base, item)` helper combining curse + high-enchant additively.
- Did NOT unify `quoteRunes`/`quoteWithMoonstone` into a block-detection algorithm — the hardcoded counts (4→115, 7→198, 6→137) cover only observed test cases.

**APP Mass (approximate)**:
- Before: ~108 (lookup table with 4 entries duplicating base+fee+rate knowledge, three magic numbers in conditionals)
- After: ~110 (added named constants add minor mass, but the lookup-table entries were eliminated and the derivation is single-sourced)
- Mass roughly unchanged; **clarity (Rule 2) clearly improved** — Rule 2 trumps APP.

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 19
Recent changes: Added isFollowUp+loyalty+cursed+highEnch branch returning 160.

The implementation has accumulated many hardcoded branches across quote(). All quote tests (1-19) are passing. Now consider whether unifying into a per-item premium computation engine is justified. Per spec: each item contributes (base + first_ins(=base*0.1) ± high_enchant(+30% if level>=5) ± curse(+50% if cursed)); then policy-wide modifiers loyalty (-20% if years>=2) and follow-up (-15% if isFollowUp) apply to the policy base; then add fee 5; round UP. Verify all 19 tests pass with such a formula before committing - check at minimum tests E7 (cursed sword + amulet = 231), E31 (cursed sword newcomer = 165), and E32 (160).

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**:
- Old `quoteWithMoonstone` was misleading (only handled 2 hardcoded item counts). Removed.
- Old `quoteRunes` returned magic numbers. Replaced with `componentGroupBase` (clearly describes computing base price for a group of same-type components).
- Added `policyBaseTotal` and `itemSurcharges` — both clearly reveal intent.
- Main `quote` function now reads top-to-bottom as the spec formula.

**APP Mass (approximate)**:
- Before: ~89 (heavy: ~12 conditionals × 4 = 48, plus many magic-number constants and 5+ early-return branches in `quote`)
- After: ~55 (4 conditionals in helpers, 2 ternaries in main quote, fewer magic numbers, more named constants)
- Net reduction in mass AND structural complexity.

**Simple Design Rules Applied**:
- Rule 1 (Tests pass): All 19 tests green before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals intent): Implementation now mirrors the spec formula directly; reading `quote()` shows base + surcharges + first-insurance - loyalty - follow-up + fee, ceil. Magic numbers (231, 195, 165, 160, 145, 95) replaced by derived computation.
- Rule 3 (No duplication): Each magic number was a duplicate encoding of the formula. Now formula exists once. Block-base logic for runes/moonstones unified via `componentGroupBase`.
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): Removed `plainSingleItemPremium`, `quoteRunes`, `quoteWithMoonstone`, the cursed-sword+amulet special case branch, and 5 hardcoded return statements. Net fewer top-level constructs while gaining clearer named intermediates.

**Anticipation Check**:
- Verified the formula against E7 (231), E31 (165), E32 (160), plus all rounding (197.5→198) and block cases (3 runes=71, 3+3=137, 2+1moonstone=88).
- Reviewed all 21 `it.todo()` entries — none test new quote-modifier combinations that would be made to pass by the engine generalization. The pending tests cover claim/insurance-sum/CLI behavior, not new quote permutations.
- The magic-number returns in the old code were themselves derived from the spec formula spelled out in the test comments (e.g., `100 + 50 + 30 − 20 + 10 − 15 + 5 = 160`); replacing the answer with the calculation that produced it is removing duplication, not anticipating.

**Tests**: 19 passed, 21 todo. All green.

**Files**:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_08-52-17_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

content.text:
Now I have full context. Let me analyze the current `claim` function:

```typescript
export function claim(_policy: Policy, _incident: Incident, _remainingCap: number): ClaimResult {
  return { payout: 400, remainingCap: 1600 };
}
```

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation**

The name `claim` clearly reveals intent — it processes an insurance claim. Keep the name.

**Step 2: Current APP Mass Calculation**

```typescript
export function claim(_policy: Policy, _incident: Incident, _remainingCap: number): ClaimResult {
  return { payout: 400, remainingCap: 1600 };
}
```

Component counts:
- Constants: 2 (400, 1600) = 2
- Bindings: 3 (_policy, _incident, _remainingCap) = 3
- Invocations: 0 = 0
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 = 0

**Total Mass: 5**

**Step 3: Evaluate Simple Design Rules**

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 20 tests passing.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: The function returns hardcoded values. The unused parameters (prefixed with `_`) clearly signal "this is a stub". This is the minimal correct implementation for the single test that exercises it. Naming is fine.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No duplication exists. Only one test exercises `claim` so there's no general pattern to extract.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Already minimal — one return statement with literal values.

**Step 4: Anticipation Check**

Looking at the upcoming `it.todo()` tests (rune claim, dragon material, deductibles, caps), they would require:
- Reading from `_policy.items`
- Reading from `_incident.damages`
- Using `_remainingCap`
- Per-item logic with conditionals
- Deductible calculation

**Any refactoring that touches these would anticipate** — e.g., extracting "deductible = 100", computing `damage - 100`, using the parameters at all, or adding any conditional structure. The next Red phase needs to introduce one of these and see a failing test. If I pre-extract a `DEDUCTIBLE = 100` constant or compute `damage - deductible` now, the next test (e.g., rune damage 200 → payout 100) would pass immediately.

**Refactoring Evaluation:**

- Naming: `claim` already clearly describes purpose. Parameters prefixed with `_` correctly signal "unused stub".
- Mass: Current mass is 5 — already near-minimal.
- Rule 2: Stub clearly reveals it's a stub.
- Rule 3: No duplication exists with a single hardcoded test case.
- Rule 4: Already minimal — single return statement.

**Any meaningful refactoring would require anticipating future tests.** For example:
- Extracting `DEDUCTIBLE = 100` and computing `500 - 100` would require reading `_incident.damages[0].amount`, which anticipates the rune test.
- Extracting an `insuranceSum` calculation would anticipate the cap tests.
- Adding any per-item iteration would anticipate the multi-item claim tests.

**No refactoring performed — code is already optimal for the current single test that exercises it.** The hardcoded stub is the textbook correct minimal implementation in TDD. The next Red phase will activate the rune test (damage 200 → payout 100), which will force the introduction of a general formula and at that point refactoring becomes meaningful.

**Step 7: Refactor Phase Complete**

- **Naming Evaluation**: `claim` keeps its name (clearly reveals intent).
- **APP Mass**: 5 (no change — no refactoring applied).
- **Refactoring**: None possible without anticipating future `it.todo()` tests. The hardcoded stub is minimal and correct for the one test that exercises `claim`. Any generalization (extracting deductible constant, reading from parameters, adding conditionals) would make at least one not-yet-active `it.todo()` test pass immediately and break the Red signal.
- **Tests**: All 20 passing.

content.text:
All 26 tests still pass.

**Step 5: Recalculate APP Mass**

New `claim`:
```
function reimbursableAmount(policy, damage) {
  const damagedItem = policy.items.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType);
  const isHighEnchant = (damagedItem?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANT_DAMAGE_THRESHOLD;
  return isHighEnchant ? damage.amount / HIGH_ENCHANT_DAMAGE_DIVISOR : damage.amount;
}

export function claim(policy, incident, _remainingCap) {
  const payout = incident.damages.reduce(
    (sum, damage) => sum + reimbursableAmount(policy, damage) - DEDUCTIBLE,
    0,
  );
  const insuranceSum = policy.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type], 0);
  const remainingCap = CAP_MULTIPLIER * insuranceSum - payout;
  return { payout, remainingCap };
}
```

Mass approximately:
- Constants: ~2 (0, the constants referenced count as bindings)
- Bindings: ~14
- Invocations: ~24 (one extra call to reimbursableAmount, but the body of the reduce shrunk)
- Conditionals: 1 = 4
- Loops: 2 = 10
- Assignments: 0

Total ≈ 54 — essentially unchanged (+1 from the helper invocation), but **Rule 2 (intent) improved**: the reduce now reads as a one-liner — "sum of (reimbursable minus deductible) for each damage" — and the helper isolates the "how much is reimbursable for one damage" concept under a self-documenting name.

**Step 6: Document Decision**

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted `reimbursableAmount(policy, damage)` helper to name the "reimbursable amount before deductible" concept
- The `claim` reduce body collapsed from 4 lines (3 intermediate bindings + return) to a single expression: `sum + reimbursableAmount(policy, damage) - DEDUCTIBLE`
- The high-enchantment 50% rule is now isolated in a function whose name (and JSDoc) describes its purpose

Benefits:
- Rule 2 (Intent): The `claim` body now reads as a domain narrative ("sum the reimbursable amounts minus the deductible across all damages"). Computational detail of the high-enchantment clause is hidden behind a named function.
- Rule 3 (Duplication): No duplication change — there was none.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): One new function added, but three local bindings (`damagedItem`, `isHighEnchant`, `reimbursable`) removed from `claim`'s scope. Net wash on element count; clarity wins.
- APP mass: essentially unchanged (~+1). This is the correct trade — Rule 2 trumps APP.

**Anticipation check**: The helper only names the existing computation. No new behavior is introduced. No `it.todo()` test would now pass that wasn't passing before — verified by all 26 tests still green and 14 todos still skipped.

**Step 7: Report Completion**

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `reimbursableAmount` helper to clarify intent in `claim`
- **Mass Change**: ~53 → ~54 (negligible; Rule 2 prioritized over APP)
- **Tests**: All 26 passing, 14 todo remaining

content.text:
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Changed `claim()` to use the `remainingCap` parameter directly: payout is capped at `Math.min(rawPayout, remainingCap)` and returned remainingCap is `remainingCap - payout`.
**Result**: All 31 tests now pass.
**Approach**: This is minimal — it uses the parameter the test passes in (e.g., `2000` for first call, `600` for second call) rather than recomputing the cap from the insurance sum. For all previously passing tests, callers already pass `2 * insuranceSum`, so `remainingCap - payout` yields the same value as before.

All 31 tests still pass.

## Step 5: APP Mass After

**Removed elements:**
- `INSURANCE_VALUES` object: 6 bindings + 6 constants (string keys treated as bindings/labels, plus 6 numeric values) ≈ 12 mass
- `CAP_MULTIPLIER`: 1 binding + 1 constant = 2 mass

**Mass reduction:** ~14 units removed from the module.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Removed unused `INSURANCE_VALUES` lookup table
- Removed unused `CAP_MULTIPLIER` constant
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): file now contains only elements that current tests demand
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): no longer suggests the implementation computes caps from policy when it actually receives them as a parameter

**Why this is not anticipation (in reverse):** these constants existed without being referenced by any active code path. They were speculative — placed in anticipation of a cap-computation feature that no current test exercises. Removing them aligns the code with the actual contract demanded by the 31 passing tests. If/when a future Red test demands cap computation from policy, those constants can be reintroduced.

**Naming evaluation:** `claim`, `rawPayout`, `payout`, `reimbursableAmount` all clearly reveal their intent. No renames needed.

## Step 7: Completion

- **Refactoring**: Removed unused `INSURANCE_VALUES` and `CAP_MULTIPLIER` (Rule 4: Fewest Elements)
- **Mass Change**: module-level mass reduced by ~14 units

content.text:
All 33 tests still pass.

**Refactor Phase Complete**

**Naming Evaluation**:
- All function names (`claim`, `quote`, `reimbursableAmount`, `policyBaseTotal`, `componentGroupBase`, `itemSurcharges`) clearly reveal their intent. No renames needed.

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted explaining variable `cappedPayout` in the `claim` function to separate two distinct concepts:
  1. Capping the raw payout at the remaining cap
  2. Rounding down in MHPCO's favor (Math.floor)
- Added a brief comment explaining the rounding rule mirrors `Math.ceil` on premiums (the conceptual symmetry from "rounding in MHPCO's favor").

**APP Mass Change**:
- Before: 27 (`const payout = Math.floor(Math.min(rawPayout, remainingCap))`)
- After: 28 (added one binding `cappedPayout`)
- Delta: +1

**Rationale**: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP. The slight mass increase is justified because the two-step structure makes the algorithm's intent clearer — first cap, then round. This separates the "limit by remaining cap" concern from the "round in MHPCO's favor" concern, which the test "payout 350.5 G rounds DOWN" highlights as a meaningful business rule.

**Anticipation Check**: No new helpers, branches, or scaffolding added for the `it.todo()` CLI or error-handling tests. The change only refactors code currently exercised by passing tests.

**Tests**: 33 passing, 7 todo. All previously passing tests remain green.

content.text:
All 34 tests pass. The reminder about tasks isn't relevant to this refactor agent work — the orchestrator manages that task. Let me calculate the new mass.

## Step 5: New APP Mass

```typescript
const { customer, steps } = JSON.parse(readFileSync(0, "utf-8"));
const results = steps.map((step: { items: Item[] }) => ({
  premium: quote(customer, step.items),
}));
process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ results }));
```

Component count:
- Constants: `0`, `"utf-8"` = 2
- Bindings: `customer`, `steps`, `results`, `step` = 4
- Invocations: `readFileSync`, `JSON.parse`, `steps.map`, `quote`, `JSON.stringify`, `process.stdout.write` = 6 → mass 12
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 1 (`map`) = 5
- Assignments: 0

Total: 2 + 4 + 12 + 0 + 5 + 0 = **23**

**Mass change: 31 → 23 (Δ −8)**

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied to `src/cli.ts`**:
- Removed anticipatory `if (step.op === "quote")` branch and the dead `return {}` fallback. No currently-active test exercises a non-quote step; the four CLI it.todo entries (claim ops, error handling) cover behavior not yet demanded. The conditional was anticipation — forbidden by the no-anticipation rule.
- Inlined the `input` binding into the `JSON.parse(readFileSync(...))` call — it was used exactly once, removing a binding without harming clarity.
- Replaced the inline `{ items: { type: string }[] }` parameter type with the existing exported `Item` type from `claim-office.ts`. Removes a small duplication (Rule 3) and aligns the CLI with the domain types.
- Converted the arrow body from `{ return { premium: ... }; }` to expression form `=> ({ premium: ... })` — fewer elements (Rule 4), same intent.

**Mass**: 31 → 23 (Δ −8)
**Naming**: All names (`customer`, `steps`, `results`, `step`) already reveal intent; kept as-is.
**Tests**: 34 passing, 6 it.todo remaining — all green.

**Why this preserves the Red signal**: The next likely activations (a `claim` step in CLI, or unknown item type / negative damage error handling) will all fail against the simplified code — there is no longer any pre-built dispatch, no error pathway, no claim handling. Red phase will see genuine failures, as required.

---

Refactor Phase Complete.
- Refactoring: removed anticipatory branch + dead fallback; inlined single-use binding; reused `Item` type; expression-body arrow.
- Mass Change: 31 → 23 (Δ −8) in `src/cli.ts`.
- Tests: 34 passing, all green.

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "CLI quote+claim sequence"
Expected: CLI processes both step types. For claim step: look up the policy from the previously created quote (by step.policy index), compute initial cap = 2*sum(insurance_values for items), track remainingCap across multiple claims on same policy, call claim() and return {payout, remainingCap}. Insurance values: sword=1000, amulet=600, staff=800, potion=400, rune=250, moonstone=250.
Implementation file: src/cli.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text:
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Extended `cli.ts` to handle claim steps. Quote steps now also stash their items in a `quotePolicies` array (indexed by quote position). For claim steps, the CLI looks up the policy by `step.policy` index, computes the initial cap on first claim against that policy as `2 * sum(INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type])`, tracks `remainingCap` in a `Map<number, number>` across successive claims on the same policy, then delegates to `claim()` and emits the `{payout, remainingCap}` it returns.
**Result**: All tests now pass (35 passing, 5 todo).
**Approach**: Minimal — the INSURANCE_VALUES table is hardcoded to the six types listed in the prompt, the policy lookup is a plain array index, and the cap tracking is the simplest structure (Map keyed by policy index) that supports the cap-exhaustion contract while only being exercised here by a single-claim scenario. No premature abstraction: did not extract a step-dispatcher or share INSURANCE_VALUES with `claim-office.ts` (the pure `claim()` function takes `remainingCap` as a parameter, so the cap table is purely a CLI concern at this stage).

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "CLI quote+claim sequence"
Expected: CLI processes both step types. For claim step: look up the policy from the previously created quote (by step.policy index), compute initial cap = 2*sum(insurance_values for items), track remainingCap across multiple claims on same policy, call claim() and return {payout, remainingCap}. Insurance values: sword=1000, amulet=600, staff=800, potion=400, rune=250, moonstone=250.
Implementation file: src/cli.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text:
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Extended `cli.ts` to handle claim steps. Quote steps now also stash their items in a `quotePolicies` array (indexed by quote position). For claim steps, the CLI looks up the policy by `step.policy` index, computes the initial cap on first claim against that policy as `2 * sum(INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type])`, tracks `remainingCap` in a `Map<number, number>` across successive claims on the same policy, then delegates to `claim()` and emits the `{payout, remainingCap}` it returns.
**Result**: All tests now pass (35 passing, 5 todo).
**Approach**: Minimal — the INSURANCE_VALUES table is hardcoded to the six types listed in the prompt, the policy lookup is a plain array index, and the cap tracking is the simplest structure (Map keyed by policy index) that supports the cap-exhaustion contract while only being exercised here by a single-claim scenario. No premature abstraction: did not extract a step-dispatcher or share INSURANCE_VALUES with `claim-office.ts` (the pure `claim()` function takes `remainingCap` as a parameter, so the cap table is purely a CLI concern at this stage).

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

content.text:
All 35 tests still pass. 

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The refactor added two helpers and removed three lines from the map body. Specifically:
- Removed: `if (!remainingCaps.has(...))` block + `remCap = remainingCaps.get(...)!` (saved 1 conditional [4] + 2 invocations [4] + 1 binding [1] + 1 invocation [2] = 11 mass from the inline hot path)
- Added in helpers: `initialCap` body (loop+constants+invocations same as before) + `remainingCapFor` (1 conditional + 1 binding + 1 invocation + 1 invocation + 1 invocation + 1 binding ≈ 12 mass)

Net mass change: roughly neutral (~0). However the per-step claim handler dropped from ~9 statements to 4, sharply improving **local readability** in the hot path — Rule 2 wins even with neutral mass.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Extracted `initialCap(items)` and `remainingCapFor(policyIndex)` helpers. The main step loop now reads as: "if quote → record and return; else → claim with current cap and update". The "lazy-init cap" knowledge is encapsulated in one function with a descriptive name.
- **Removed non-null assertion** (`!` after `.get(...)`): the helper returns `number` directly, eliminating the unsafe assertion.
- **Mass change**: roughly neutral overall (~0 net), but mass *redistributed* — the hot path in `map` callback is leaner.
- **Tests**: 35 passing, 5 todo (unchanged).

**Not refactored (intentional):**
- The duplication between `INSURANCE_VALUES` here and `BASE_PRICES` in `claim-office.ts` is conceptual but **not extracted** — doing so would anticipate the validation tests (`it.todo` for unknown item type). The current `INSURANCE_VALUES` is only what today's CLI tests demand.
- Did not introduce a `Policy`/`PolicyTracker` class — would add elements (Rule 4) for behavior no current test exercises.

## Step 7: Report

Refactor Phase Complete.
- **Refactoring**: extracted `initialCap` and `remainingCapFor` helpers in `cli.ts`; removed non-null assertion.
- **Mass change**: ~neutral total; hot-path mass reduced.
- **Tests**: 35 passing, 5 todo. All green.

content.text:
Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added a per-quote-step item-type validation loop in `src/cli.ts`. Before invoking `quote()`, the CLI checks each item's `type` against the known set (the keys of `INSURANCE_VALUES`: sword, amulet, staff, potion, rune, moonstone). On the first unknown type, it writes a one-line error to stderr and exits with status 1 — guaranteeing no stdout is written.

**Result**: All tests now pass (37 passing, 3 todo).

**Approach**: Minimal. Reused the existing `INSURANCE_VALUES` lookup (already the source of truth for known types in `cli.ts`) as the validation predicate — no new constant, no new error class. Validation lives inside the `map` callback right where the quote step is handled, and `process.exit(1)` short-circuits before the `process.stdout.write` line at the bottom of the file, satisfying the "no results on stdout" requirement.

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/cli.ts
Passing tests: 37
Recent changes: CLI validates quote item types; exits non-zero with stderr on unknown.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete

**Naming Evaluation (first priority):**
- Existing names (`quotePolicies`, `remainingCaps`, `initialCap`, `remainingCapFor`, `INSURANCE_VALUES`) all clearly reveal intent — no rename needed.
- New helpers named to reveal intent: `assertKnownItemTypes` (states the precondition being enforced) and `failWith` (clear single-purpose CLI error sink).

**Refactoring Applied (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):**

Extracted the inline validation loop introduced in the recent Green phase into two small helpers:

- `failWith(message)` — single place that owns the "write to stderr + exit non-zero" CLI failure convention.
- `assertKnownItemTypes(items)` — encapsulates the precondition that every quoted item's `type` exists in `INSURANCE_VALUES`.

This separates the "validate input" concern from the "dispatch quote/claim" concern in the `map` callback. The callback now reads as a sequence of intent: `assertKnownItemTypes(step.items)` → `isFollowUp` → push policy → return premium.

**APP Mass (the recent-change region — quote branch + validation):**

Before:
- Constants: 2 (`"Unknown item type: ..."` string, `1` for exit code) → 2
- Bindings: 5 (`step`, `item`, `step.items`, `INSURANCE_VALUES`, plus implicit) → 5
- Invocations: 3 (`stderr.write`, `process.exit`, `in`) → 6
- Conditionals: 1 (`if`) → 4
- Loops: 1 (`for…of`) → 5
- Total local mass: **22**

After (call site):
- Bindings: 1 (`step.items`) → 1
- Invocations: 1 (`assertKnownItemTypes`) → 2
- Total local mass at call site: **3**

The two new helpers themselves carry ~20 mass internally, so the total program mass is roughly equal — but the *call site* mass dropped from 22 to 3, which is the win for Rule 2: the main dispatch flow is now much easier to scan.

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete

**Naming Evaluation (first priority):**
- Existing names (`quotePolicies`, `remainingCaps`, `initialCap`, `remainingCapFor`, `INSURANCE_VALUES`) all clearly reveal intent — no rename needed.
- New helpers named to reveal intent: `assertKnownItemTypes` (states the precondition being enforced) and `failWith` (clear single-purpose CLI error sink).

**Refactoring Applied (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):**

Extracted the inline validation loop introduced in the recent Green phase into two small helpers:

- `failWith(message)` — single place that owns the "write to stderr + exit non-zero" CLI failure convention.
- `assertKnownItemTypes(items)` — encapsulates the precondition that every quoted item's `type` exists in `INSURANCE_VALUES`.

This separates the "validate input" concern from the "dispatch quote/claim" concern in the `map` callback. The callback now reads as a sequence of intent: `assertKnownItemTypes(step.items)` → `isFollowUp` → push policy → return premium.

**APP Mass (the recent-change region — quote branch + validation):**

Before:
- Constants: 2 (`"Unknown item type: ..."` string, `1` for exit code) → 2
- Bindings: 5 (`step`, `item`, `step.items`, `INSURANCE_VALUES`, plus implicit) → 5
- Invocations: 3 (`stderr.write`, `process.exit`, `in`) → 6
- Conditionals: 1 (`if`) → 4
- Loops: 1 (`for…of`) → 5
- Total local mass: **22**

After (call site):
- Bindings: 1 (`step.items`) → 1
- Invocations: 1 (`assertKnownItemTypes`) → 2
- Total local mass at call site: **3**

The two new helpers themselves carry ~20 mass internally, so the total program mass is roughly equal — but the *call site* mass dropped from 22 to 3, which is the win for Rule 2: the main dispatch flow is now much easier to scan.

**No anticipation:** Reviewed the three `it.todo()` entries (claim-side validations: item-not-in-policy, negative amount, too-many-damage-entries). None of those would be satisfied by `assertKnownItemTypes` — it is scoped strictly to today's passing test (unknown item type during quote). No claim-side validation, no generic "validate step" engine, no shared validation pipeline introduced.

All 39 tests pass.

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST priority)**:
- `assertNonNegativeDamageAmounts` (new helper): Name clearly reveals intent — checks that each damage amount in the incident is non-negative. Keep as is.
- `quotePolicies` (existing): The "quote" prefix was redundant — these ARE the policies once stored. Renamed to `policies` for clarity (Rule 2).

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Renamed `quotePolicies` → `policies` (Rule 2, Reveals Intent). The previous name was awkwardly prefixed; the array stores policies (each is a list of insured items), and the surrounding code already uses `policyItems`, `policy`, `policyIndex` — uniform terminology now.

**Mass Calculation**:

`assertNonNegativeDamageAmounts` (new helper, focal point):
- Bindings: 2 (`incident`, `damage`) = 2
- Invocations: 1 (`failWith`) = 2
- Conditionals: 1 (`if`) = 4
- Loops: 1 (`for...of`) = 5
- Constants: 0 in body (template parts inline)
Mass: ~13 — minimal for the responsibility. Cannot reduce without merging with another assertion (which would hurt Rule 2).

Total file mass unchanged by the rename (Δ 0). The improvement is purely Rule 2 (clarity).

**Refactorings Considered and Rejected**:
- **Merge `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy` + `assertNonNegativeDamageAmounts`** into a single damages-validation pass (Rule 3 win, mass reduction). Rejected: Rule 2 trumps Rule 3 — two named single-responsibility helpers read more clearly than one combined god-function. Also risks anticipating the remaining `it.todo()` ("more damage entries than policy covers"), which is a distinct check.
- **Extract `handleQuote`/`handleClaim`** from the main `.map` callback. Rejected: the branches are 4 lines each and read cleanly inline. Adds invocations (mass cost) without meaningful clarity gain.
- **Add item-type context to the negative-amount error message**. Rejected: behavior change, not refactoring; no test demands it. Would anticipate quality requirements not in the spec.

**Anticipation Check (No Anticipation Rule)**:
- Verified the one remaining `it.todo()`: "CLI: claim contains more damage entries of a type than the policy covers". The current code does NOT count damage entries per type — if that test were activated tomorrow, it would Red (fail) correctly. No anticipation occurred.

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Renamed `quotePolicies` → `policies` (Rule 2, Reveals Intent). The previous name was awkwardly prefixed; the array stores policies (each is a list of insured items), and the surrounding code already uses `policyItems`, `policy`, `policyIndex` — uniform terminology now.

**Mass Calculation**:

`assertNonNegativeDamageAmounts` (new helper, focal point):
- Bindings: 2 (`incident`, `damage`) = 2
- Invocations: 1 (`failWith`) = 2
- Conditionals: 1 (`if`) = 4

- Constants: 0 in body (template parts inline)

Rejected: Rule 2 trumps Rule 3 — two named single-responsibility helpers read more clearly than one combined god-function. Also risks anticipating the remaining `it.todo()` ("more damage entries than policy covers"), which is a distinct check.
- **Extract `handleQuote`/`handleClaim`** from the main `.map` callback. Rejected: the branches are 4 lines each and read cleanly inline. Adds invocations (mass cost) without meaningful clarity gain.
- **Add item-type context to the negative-amount error message**. Rejected: behavior change, not refactoring; no test demands it. Would anticipate quality requirements not in the spec.

**Anticipation Check (No Anticipation Rule)**:

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted `countBy<T>(values, key)` generic helper in `src/cli.ts` to remove duplicated "count occurrences of a key in a collection" logic that appeared twice in `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy` (once for policy items, once for damages).

**Naming Evaluation**:
- All function names (`assertKnownItemTypes`, `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy`, `assertNonNegativeDamageAmounts`, `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy`, `failWith`, `initialCap`, `remainingCapFor`) clearly reveal intent — kept as-is.
- New `countBy<T>` follows the well-known Lodash/Ruby `count_by` convention — intent is self-evident.

**APP Mass — `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy`**:
- Before: ~44 (3 loops, 2 inline count-build patterns, multiple Map invocations + `??` defaults)
- After: ~24 (1 loop, 2 helper invocations, 1 conditional)
- The helper `countBy` itself: ~16 mass (1 loop, 1 invocation, basic bindings)
- Net change: removed one full loop and two `Map.get`/`??`/`set` sequences from the caller; the extracted helper unifies the pattern.

**Rule Application**:
- Rule 1 (Tests pass): All 40 tests still green.
- Rule 2 (Reveals intent): `countBy(policyItems, item => item.type)` reads as English; clearer than the manual loop.
- Rule 3 (No duplication): Two near-identical count-building loops collapsed into one helper.
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): Added one helper function (3 lines of "necessary plumbing"), but eliminated 2 loops + 4 Map operations from the caller; net reduction in code elements.

**Anticipation check**: `countBy` is generic but used in exactly one place, called twice. No second consumer was added speculatively. The generic type parameter exists only because the two call sites already operate on different types (`Item` and `Damage`) — it serves today's tests, not tomorrow's.

**Tests**: All 40 passing.
